state_system.purpose: Unified state rules for loading, empty, success, error, disabled, and live-progress experiences across the site.
core_principles: Every interactive area must communicate its current state clearly.
States must reduce uncertainty, not increase it.
Users should always know what is happening, what happened, and what to do next.
Visual feedback must be timely and consistent.
States must be informative without becoming noisy.

default_state.description: The neutral baseline state before user action or data retrieval.
rules: Default states must establish context.
Do not leave large blank areas without explanation.
Use placeholder text or helper copy where users may otherwise feel lost.
examples: Enter your printer settings to estimate the true monthly cost.
Use filters to narrow down printer results.
Select a tab to manage products, blog posts, or scraping tools.

loading_state.description: Indicates data is being fetched or work is in progress.
principles: Show that the system is working.
Match the loading layout to the final content shape when possible.

rules: Use near the affected area.
Do not rely on a lone spinner for large content regions when skeletons are better.
Skeletons should roughly mirror final structure.
Avoid flashy skeleton animations that distract.
Prevent layout jumps when real content arrives.
Use only when a richer loading treatment is unnecessary.
Pair with subtle visual feedback where possible.

copy_rules: If loading may take longer, indicate what is happening.
examples: Loading products…
Calculating cost…
Fetching comparison data…

empty_state.description: Indicates that there is no content to display in the current context.
principles: Differentiate between 'nothing exists yet' and 'no results found'.
Use an icon or visual cue only if it supports clarity.

no_data_yet.description: The system has no created content yet.
examples: No products have been added yet.
No blog posts exist yet.
No printers match your filters.
No products matched your search.

not_selected_yet.description: The user has not started the relevant workflow.
examples: You have not added any printers to compare yet.
Choose a printer type to begin.

visual_rules: Empty states must be centered or clearly framed within the affected area.
Use structured spacing and readable copy.
Provide one clear next step when possible.

success_state.description: Confirms that an action completed successfully.
principles: Do not over-celebrate routine admin actions.
Where appropriate, indicate the next possible step.
visual_rules: Icons may support the message but must not be the only signal.
examples: Product saved successfully.
Blog post published.
Calculation updated.

error_state.description: Communicates that something failed or needs correction.
principles: Explain the issue in plain language.
When possible, explain how to recover.

rules: Attach error feedback near the relevant control when possible.
Use visible border/state changes plus readable text.
Do not rely on color alone.
Replace broken blank states with explicit error UI.
Offer retry when appropriate.
Explain whether the action partially completed or failed fully.
Keep destructive failure messaging especially clear.

examples: Could not load products. Please try again.
Login failed. Check your password and try again.
Please enter a valid non-negative number.

disabled_state.description: Indicates an action is currently unavailable.
rules: Disabled controls must look non-interactive.
The reason for disabled state should be understandable from context or helper text.
Do not silently disable critical actions without explanation.

partial_state.description: Used when only some content is available or some work completed.
rules: Indicate what succeeded and what remains missing.
Use this for mixed-result admin operations or partially loaded dashboards when relevant.
examples: Some products loaded, but blog data could not be fetched.
3 of 5 scrape tasks completed.

live_progress_state.description: Represents work that is actively updating over time.
rules: Show live progress in a dedicated area.
Use monospace logs if appropriate.
Auto-scroll intelligently when new lines are appended.
Preserve user ability to inspect previous output.
Use polite live region behavior for accessibility when needed.

state_layout_rules: States must render within the affected component or section whenever possible.
Do not redirect the user's attention to unrelated parts of the page for critical state feedback.
Keep state UI aligned with the component hierarchy.
state_copy_rules: Explain the outcome, not internal implementation.
Where needed, include the next step.
Avoid generic phrases like 'Something went wrong' without context.
state_visual_rules: Use consistent iconography if icons are used.
Do not rely on color alone.
Use spacing and framing so states feel intentional, not like broken layouts.
State styling must remain consistent across pages.

loading: Primary action may show spinner and temporarily disable repeated submission.
Do not hide the context of the action while loading.
empty: Provide one recovery or next-step CTA where possible.
error: Provide retry or corrective CTA when appropriate.
success: Allow continuation or return to workflow if relevant.

animation_rules: State transitions may be subtle and fast.
Do not use large theatrical animations for admin or utility workflows.
Prioritize stability and clarity over visual flourish.

rules: Dynamic status updates should use aria-live where appropriate.
Messages must remain understandable without icons or color alone.
Focus should move predictably after major state changes only when it genuinely helps.

anti_patterns: Errors that do not explain recovery
Success banners that feel louder than the task itself
Disabled buttons with no understandable reason
Logs that update invisibly below the fold
implementation_notes_for_ai: Always identify the likely states of a component before redesigning it.
Create structured empty, loading, success, and error UI instead of leaving placeholders.
Use the same state language patterns across the site.
When uncertain, prefer explicit helpful feedback over minimalist ambiguity.
